abstract: Solar cells based on alkyl ammonium metal halides offer a compelling blend of high power conversion efficiency and ease of fabrication, positioning them as prime candidates for affordable thin-film photovoltaics. However, their tendency to degrade under sustained sunlight remains a critical hurdle. In this work, we demonstrate that this degradation is quasi-reversible and can be significantly mitigated by optimizing the device's operating environment. Using in operando electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), we investigated lead triiodide perovskite devices featuring methylammonium (MA), formamidinium (FA), and mixed MA(x)FA(1-x) cations. Variable-temperature EIS measurements revealed that the diffusion coefficient for MA ions is substantially higher than for FA ions. Complementary structural analysis via powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) showed that MAPbI3 undergoes a lattice expansion and a tetragonal-to-cubic phase transition at typical operating temperatures. Based on these findings, we propose that the accelerated degradation of MAPbI3 under sunlight is driven by thermally activated fast ion transport coupled with this lattice-expanding phase transition—both of which are triggered by the infrared component of the solar spectrum. By addressing these thermal effects, we demonstrate that device stability and operating lifetimes are dramatically improved when using white-light LEDs, infrared cut-off filters, or active cooling.
